judge.justification: Both outputs are closely matched across all four dimensions, but A edges out B on ranking calibration — the expected behavior calls for Nash Full Stack (job 7), Mariana Minerals (job 8), Kognitos (job 5), and Homebase AI (job 1) to rank highest, with Amazon (job 2) and Apple (job 6) as generic mid-tier, and iOS roles (jobs 3/4) lowest. A's ordering (92, 72, 28, 25, 90, 70, 88, 85) correctly places the AI-native and full-stack roles at the top and iOS roles at the bottom, and the spread between tiers is more decisive and better calibrated. B's ordering (88, 72, 22, 18, 86, 68, 90, 87) is also reasonable but compresses the top-tier scores too tightly and places job 7 (Nash Full Stack) above job 1 (Homebase AI) and job 5 (Kognitos) by only marginal differences, slightly muddying the ranking signal. A also provides more substantive skill_gaps reasoning (e.g., noting Java and AWS ecosystem depth for Amazon, C++ for Apple), which adds analytical value even if those gaps are debatable. Impact quality is comparable between both — both reference the 1,000+ orders, 95%+ accuracy, and 50+ customers metrics appropriately.
